metadata.summary: Concentrate every Python tool's configuration in pyproject.toml. Forbid setup.py, setup.cfg, tox.ini, .flake8, requirements*.txt for new code. One file = one place AI agents read, parse, and mutate.

content.title: `pyproject.toml` as the Single Source of Configuration
content.summary: Concentrate every Python tool's configuration in `pyproject.toml` — `[build-system]`, `[project]` (PEP 621), `[tool.uv]`, `[tool.ruff]`, type-checker (`[tool.ty]` or `[tool.mypy]`), `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`, `[tool.coverage.run]`, `[tool.bandit]`, `[tool.mutmut]`. Forbid `setup.py`, `setup.cfg`, `tox.ini`, `.flake8`, `requirements*.txt` for Python tooling on new code. One file = one place AI agents have to read, parse, and mutate.

why.p: PEP 621 standardizes project metadata in pyproject.toml; Hatchling, setuptools, flit, and uv all consume that table directly. Splitting config into `setup.cfg` + `tox.ini` + `.flake8` + `requirements.txt` is a 2017-era pattern that produces silent inconsistencies — the agent edits one file, forgets the other, and the repo enters a half-migrated state. A single TOML file is parseable, diffable, and machine-mutable; agents reason about it more reliably than about a swarm of legacy config files.

list.item: Every new Python project, library, service, or CLI.
Migrating any project where `setup.cfg`, `tox.ini`, or `.flake8` still exists alongside `pyproject.toml`.
Tooling-heavy repos with ruff + pytest + coverage + bandit + mutmut all configured.
Any repo touched by a coding agent — fewer files to discover means less drift.
Build systems with truly exotic native-extension logic where `setup.py` carries imperative build steps that TOML cannot express — keep `setup.py` minimal and put metadata in `[project]`.
Vendored read-only mirrors of upstream projects whose layout you cannot change.
`tox` users who deliberately keep `tox.ini` for parallelism config that pyproject's `[tool.tox]` does not yet support — the trade-off is local; document it.

section.name: single-file-rule
section.p: Every Python tool that supports pyproject.toml must be configured there. Legacy alternatives are deleted, not co-existed-with. A one-line shell check fails the build if any forbidden file reappears.

rule.statement: The repo contains zero of: `setup.py` (unless required for native build steps), `setup.cfg`, `.flake8`, `pytest.ini`, `tox.ini`, `mypy.ini`, `requirements.txt` (for first-party deps). All replaced by sections inside `pyproject.toml`.
rule.rationale: PEP 621 provides the standard metadata table; PEP 518 provides the build-system table. Every modern Python tool reads `pyproject.toml`. Multiple config files create silent conflicts an agent will miss when editing one and forgetting another.

example.code: # in CI: fail if any forbidden file exists test ! -f setup.cfg && \ test ! -f tox.ini && \ test ! -f .flake8 && \ test ! -f pytest.ini && \ test ! -f mypy.ini && \ test ! -f requirements.txt
example.note: One liner blocks the legacy files from sneaking back in via copy-paste.

example.code: . ├── pyproject.toml # has [tool.ruff] ├── setup.cfg # ALSO has [flake8] section ├── .flake8 # AND a third copy of lint rules ├── tox.ini # AND test config diverging from pytest └── requirements-dev.txt # deps not in pyproject.toml
example.note: Four config files, four sources of truth, every agent edit drifts one of them.

antipattern.description: Adding a new tool's config to a fresh `.toolnamerc` file because "the README said to".
antipattern.reason: If the tool reads `pyproject.toml` (it almost certainly does in 2026), use `[tool.<name>]`. The dotfile reintroduces the same drift problem the rule is preventing.

section.name: canonical-sections
section.p: The set of TOML tables every modern Python project should use, and what each one replaces.

list.item: `[build-system]` — PEP 518 build backend (hatchling, setuptools, flit, etc.).
`[project]` — PEP 621 metadata: name, version, requires-python, dependencies, optional-dependencies.
`[tool.ruff]` and `[tool.ruff.lint]` / `[tool.ruff.format]` — replaces flake8 + isort + black + pyupgrade.
`[tool.pytest.ini_options]` — replaces pytest.ini and tox.ini test sections.

rule.statement: When a repo uses pytest + ruff + coverage + a type checker, `pyproject.toml` MUST contain `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`, `[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.coverage.run]`, and exactly one of `[tool.ty]` / `[tool.mypy]` / `[tool.pyright]`. Missing sections mean the tool is reading either defaults or a legacy file.
rule.rationale: If a tool runs without its TOML section present, it is either using global defaults (silent risk) or an undeclared config file (drift). Both are caught by checking the table is present.

example.code:
[build-system] requires = ["hatchling"] build-backend = "hatchling.build"
[project] name = "my-svc" version = "1.0.0" requires-python = ">=3.12" dependencies = ["fastapi>=0.115"]
[project.optional-dependencies] dev = ["pytest>=9", "ruff>=0.15", "ty"]
[tool.ruff] line-length = 100
[tool.ruff.lint] select = ["E", "F", "I", "B", "UP", "SIM", "T20"]
[tool.pytest.ini_options] minversion = "9.0" addopts = "-ra --strict-markers --strict-config" testpaths = ["tests"]
[tool.coverage.run] branch = true source = ["src"]
[tool.ty.environment] python-version = "3.12"
example.note: Every relevant tool has its table. No setup.cfg, no .flake8, no pytest.ini.

antipattern.description: Configuring `ruff` in pyproject.toml AND keeping `.flake8` for "the parts ruff doesn't cover yet".
antipattern.reason: Ruff covers all flake8 plugins as `--select` codes; if a rule is missing, request it upstream or use a single dedicated tool. Two parallel lint configs guarantee the agent edits one and breaks the other.

template.purpose: Reference pyproject.toml with all common tool sections wired up.
One-line CI check that fails if forbidden config files exist.
